question: What is the key difference between `loadComponent` and `loadChildren` in Angular routing?
options: loadComponent is for lazy-loading standalone components; loadChildren is for lazy-loading NgModule-based route sets
loadComponent is synchronous; loadChildren is asynchronous
loadComponent requires a resolver; loadChildren does not
They are identical — loadChildren is just the older alias for loadComponent

question: What does a route resolver do?
options: It resolves route conflicts when two routes match the same path
It fetches data before the route activates, making the data available immediately when the component renders
It pre-renders the component on the server before sending it to the client
It caches the route so subsequent navigations don't require a new HTTP call

question: A user navigates from /users/1 to /users/2. The UserDetailComponent is reused (not destroyed). You read the user ID using `this.route.snapshot.paramMap.get('id')` in ngOnInit. What happens?
options: The component correctly loads user 2 because snapshot is always current
Angular throws a navigation error because the component is reused
The component still shows user 1's data because snapshot was captured at init and never updates
The component re-initializes automatically, so ngOnInit runs again with the new snapshot

question: What does `PreloadAllModules` do when used as a preloading strategy?
options: It loads all lazy modules eagerly at startup, defeating the purpose of lazy loading
After the app's initial load completes, it fetches all lazy chunks in the background so future navigations feel instant
It only preloads modules that are currently visible in the viewport
It preloads a module only when the user hovers over a routerLink pointing to it

question: In a nested routes setup, where does the child component render?
options: In the root <router-outlet> of AppComponent
Inside a <router-outlet> in the parent route's component template
As a sibling element next to the parent component
Nested routes are not supported — each route must have its own top-level outlet
